title: Understanding the Ancient Maya: Unraveling the Secrets of Lidar Surveys in Guatemala

lesson.learn: In 2020, a team of researchers at the University of Houston led by Dr. George D. Hasenstab utilized lidar technology in conjunction with machine learning algorithms to map the Petén Basin in Guatemala, an area inhabited by the Maya civilization for over 1,100 years, from approximately 300 BCE to 900 CE. Covering an area of 12,600 square kilometers, the Petén Basin is one of the largest Maya archaeological regions, with over 1,500 recorded sites. Dr. Hasenstab's team collected an impressive 1.3 billion data points using lidar, which were then analyzed to precisely reconstruct the layout of the ancient city. This groundbreaking discovery underscores the significance of lidar technology in archaeology, enabling researchers to uncover previously unknown sites and artifacts with unparalleled accuracy. By leveraging the precision of lidar, researchers can create highly detailed 3D models of the environment, akin to using a high-precision camera with a vast field of view, capturing subtle topographic changes in the landscape. This capability allows for the identification of hidden structures and artifacts, including ancient roads, canals, and even palaces. For instance, a 2018 lidar survey in the largest Maya city discovered to date, located in the Belize River Valley, revealed a sprawling metropolis covering over 170 square kilometers, redefining our previous understanding of ancient Maya civilizations. The fusion of lidar data with machine learning algorithms has opened new avenues for researchers to study and analyze the complexities of ancient societies, such as agricultural practices, trade routes, and social organization.
lesson.simpler: Lidar technology generates detailed 3D models of the environment by emitting laser pulses, allowing researchers to reconstruct the layout of ancient cities without manual excavation. This innovative approach complements traditional archaeological methods, enabling researchers to obtain a more comprehensive understanding of ancient cultures.
lesson.keyTakeaway: The integration of lidar technology in archaeology has enabled researchers to identify previously unknown sites and artifacts, offering a more nuanced understanding of ancient cultures and societies. By leveraging the symbiotic relationship between lidar data and machine learning algorithms, researchers can uncover valuable insights into the complexity and organization of ancient societies.
lesson.deeperDive: One of the key advantages of lidar technology lies in its ability to analyze vast datasets with unprecedented precision. By integrating lidar data with machine learning algorithms, researchers can identify patterns in city architecture, track changes in agriculture and trade routes, and even reconstruct the original landscape. For instance, a 2015 lidar survey in Cambodia revealed multiple layers of ancient temple complexes buried beneath the surface, demonstrating the potential of lidar technology in unearthing the secrets of the past. Furthermore, the precision of lidar technology permits the detection of features as small as 10-15 centimeters in height, underscoring its value in archaeological research.
lesson.funFact: Lidar technology has enabled researchers to pinpoint features previously imperceptible, such as ancient roads and canals, by leveraging its exceptional precision to capture 3D data from varying environments.
